pitches.Splitter: Gausman's calling card — when the velo separation off his four-seam is sharp, he's unhittable.
pitches.Curveball: Berríos's signature pitch — heavy depth from a high slot, his best swing-and-miss offering.
pitches.Four-seam Fastball: Command up in the zone is the calling card for the staff's strike-throwers — average velo plays up when the location is precise.
pitches.Two-seam Fastball / Sinker: Corbin lives off his sinker against lefties — when the run is sharp, he steals called strikes on the inner half. Two-seamers also come standard in the bullpen toolkit, usually paired with a slider.
pitches.Cutter: Scherzer's secondary fastball when he's right — late-moving, in on lefties' hands. The other cutter throwers on the staff use it the same way: a fastball with just enough movement to break a barrel late.
pitches.Slider: The shape that defines modern bat-misser arsenals across the league. Cease headlines the staff at his best; the rest of the bullpen carry tighter, harder sliders that play off their fastballs.
pitches.Sweeper: The horizontal-break slider variant that's reshaped MLB pitch mixes in recent seasons. The Jays staff hasn't tilted heavily into it — the breaking-ball shapes lean more vertical than horizontal.
pitches.Slurve / Hybrid: A breaking ball with both lateral sweep and vertical depth — neither pure slider nor pure curve. Rare across MLB; the current staff doesn't carry a true slurve as a primary offering.
pitches.Changeup: Gausman's splitter does the staff's changeup work at the top of the rotation — it behaves as a changeup with extra dive. Among the rest, the cleanest straight changeup belongs to Varland.
pitches.Knuckleball: Not in any current Blue Jays pitcher's arsenal. The Jays' last knuckleballer was R.A. Dickey, whose 2012-2015 run included a Cy Young-winning season.<!-- noscan -->
pitches.Eephus: A 50-mph lob — a curiosity pitch that surfaces from position-player relief outings rather than from real pitchers. Nobody on the staff carries one.

665489.recentNote: On-base machine — .386 OBP carrying a quiet slugging line.
665489.read: Vlad's surface line looks merely good (.287/.386/.372), but the shape is unusual: the OBP is elite for a corner bat while the slug sits at .372 — well below his career mark. The walks are real and sustainable; the missing damage on contact is the question. Three home runs through 188 ABs reads like batted-ball variance rather than a swing change. If the slug climbs toward .500 as the weather warms, the OPS jumps from fine to middle-of-the-order anchor in a hurry. Watch the barrel rate — that's the leading indicator that the power is coming back, not the home run count.

contextNotes.avg: Right in line with his career norm. The hits are landing.
contextNotes.obp: Top-tier walk rate is doing the heavy lifting here.
contextNotes.slg: Well below career — three HR through 188 AB is the gap. Expect regression upward.
contextNotes.ops: Pulled down by the slug. Watch this number move when the power returns.
contextNotes.hr: Pace is light for a middle-of-the-order bat. Contact quality, not approach, is the question.

623993.detail: Left shoulder subluxation — diving catch attempt in right field 4/3 at Yankee Stadium; structural MRI clean but the swing has been slow to come back.
623993.eta: Atkins 5/22: rehab assignment to begin once he's cleared to swing with full intent — "weeks, not days."

overview.headline: The bats are doing the work, the rotation is doing the surviving
paragraphs: The shape of this season is already clear: the offense is doing the work, and the rotation is doing the surviving. A top-five run-scoring environment is masking a starting staff that's been a half-rotation short since opening week, and the bullpen has caught more high-leverage innings than anyone in the organization planned for. The record is fine. The way it was earned is the story.

The lineup has been the constant. The top of the order is grinding at-bats deep, the team OBP sits comfortably above league average, and the situational hitting — runners on, two outs, all the categories that tend to wobble — has held up. There's a real argument that this is the most patient Jays lineup since 2021. The slug will follow as the weather warms; the underlying contact quality says the home runs are coming.

The rotation is the question that won't go away. Innings-per-start has been a chronic problem, and the back end has been a revolving door of spot starters and openers. Until someone behind the front two strings together six healthy starts in a row, every off-day is going to feel like a small mercy and every doubleheader is going to feel like a stress test. The trade-deadline conversation is going to start with starting pitching and end there.

Defensively, the up-the-middle work has been quietly excellent. The middle infield is converting the routine play at a high rate and the outfield range numbers are tracking above last year. None of it shows up on a scoreboard, but it's the reason the FIP-ERA gap on the staff is as small as it is. The fielders are saving runs the pitchers don't quite earn.

Where this goes next: if the rotation can stabilize at all — even a competent fifth starter, even one full month of health — this team has a path to a 90-win pace. If it doesn't, the offense will keep winning the games it's supposed to win and lose the ones it shouldn't have to. The fault line is the same one it's been since spring.

ctx.hitting.runs: Bottom-third in MLB so far — biggest year-over-year drop-off of any offense, and the single biggest reason the rotation's good work is getting wasted.
ctx.hitting.avg: Right around league average. The hits are landing at a fine rate; the damage on contact is the missing piece.
ctx.hitting.obp: Below league average despite the top of the order grinding at-bats. The bottom third of the lineup isn't getting on base, and it's compressing the offense.
ctx.hitting.slg: The slug tells the story — well below league average and a full hundred points off last year's mark. The ball isn't leaving the yard.
ctx.hitting.ops: Bottom quartile in MLB. The OBP holds up; the slug is what drags the line down.
ctx.hitting.hr: Mid-pack on paper, but the home-run rate per fly ball is the lowest in the AL. Either the weather warms or the swing decisions change — something has to give.
ctx.pitching.era: Top-third in MLB. The rotation has been the unsung anchor of this team, even with the back-end churn.
ctx.pitching.whip: Above-average traffic management. Hits and walks are kept in check; the staff doesn't beat itself.
ctx.pitching.k9: Top-five in MLB. The strikeout stuff is real and it's the load-bearing trait on the pitching side.
ctx.pitching.bb9: Top-ten command. Free passes are not the problem on this staff — a reassuring foundation when the bats aren't producing.

strengths: Patient at-bat work at the top: Vlad and the front of the order are walking at career-high rates, and the team's pitches-per-PA mark is in the AL's top five. The lineup is making starters work even on the nights the hits don't fall.

Rotation strike-throwing: the starters' BB/9 is a top-ten mark in MLB. Gausman is the obvious anchor with Berríos out, and the back-end fillers have largely avoided the walk-fueled blowup innings that sink staffs.
Strikeout stuff on the mound: a top-five K/9 means even on the rough nights the staff has an out it can manufacture. That's the trait that keeps the high-leverage innings winnable.

Up-the-middle defense: the middle infield is converting the routine play at a high rate and the outfield range numbers are tracking above last year. None of it scores runs, but it's the reason the FIP-ERA gap is as small as it is.

Bullpen leverage management: the high-leverage arms are posting sub-3 ERAs and the back of the pen has held leads at an above-average clip. With the rotation short on length, that endurance is keeping winnable games winnable.
softspots: Power outage: the team home-run total puts the Jays in the bottom third of MLB, well off last year's pace. Until the slug climbs, every offensive night needs three or four hits to chain together rather than one swing to clear the bases.

Bottom-of-the-order OBP: spots six through nine are running an OBP below .300, and it's compressing the lineup — the top of the order keeps coming up with the bases empty. Either the bench gets a shake-up or the next call-up gets a long look.

Innings-per-start from the back end: the rotation behind the front two has averaged barely over five innings a start, which is why the bullpen has caught more high-leverage work than planned. The seventh inning is where this team is most vulnerable.

Two-strike contact quality: the team's two-strike whiff rate is bottom-ten in MLB. Hitters are getting deep into counts and then giving the at-bat back — that's the trait that turns a patient approach into a frustrating one.
Runners-in-scoring-position slug: the team's RISP SLG sits well below the overall SLG, which itself is well below average. The chances are being created; the damage with runners on isn't happening.
